rcomplex 0.1.0
  • Reference
  • Articles
    • Comparative co-expression analysis with rcomplex

Comparative co-expression analysis with rcomplex

Source: vignettes/rcomplex-tutorial.Rmd
rcomplex-tutorial.Rmd

This vignette demonstrates a complete rcomplex analysis using expression data from eight Pooideae grass species spanning four phylogenetic pairs, each containing one annual and one perennial species:

Species Code Life cycle Pair
Brachypodium distachyon BDIS Annual Brachypodium
Brachypodium sylvaticum BSYL Perennial Brachypodium
Hordeum vulgare HVUL Annual Hordeum
Hordeum jubatum HJUB Perennial Hordeum
Briza maxima BMAX Annual Briza
Briza media (crossmapped to BMAX) BMED Perennial Briza
Vulpia bromoides VBRO Annual Festuca/Vulpia
Festuca pratensis FPRA Perennial Festuca/Vulpia

The dataset is a 500-HOG subset of VST-normalized RNA-seq from the EVOTREE project, stored as SummarizedExperiment objects.

1. Data preparation 

library(rcomplex)
library(SummarizedExperiment)
#> Loading required package: MatrixGenerics
#> Loading required package: matrixStats
#> 
#> Attaching package: 'MatrixGenerics'
#> The following objects are masked from 'package:matrixStats':
#> 
#>     colAlls, colAnyNAs, colAnys, colAvgsPerRowSet, colCollapse,
#>     colCounts, colCummaxs, colCummins, colCumprods, colCumsums,
#>     colDiffs, colIQRDiffs, colIQRs, colLogSumExps, colMadDiffs,
#>     colMads, colMaxs, colMeans2, colMedians, colMins, colOrderStats,
#>     colProds, colQuantiles, colRanges, colRanks, colSdDiffs, colSds,
#>     colSums2, colTabulates, colVarDiffs, colVars, colWeightedMads,
#>     colWeightedMeans, colWeightedMedians, colWeightedSds,
#>     colWeightedVars, rowAlls, rowAnyNAs, rowAnys, rowAvgsPerColSet,
#>     rowCollapse, rowCounts, rowCummaxs, rowCummins, rowCumprods,
#>     rowCumsums, rowDiffs, rowIQRDiffs, rowIQRs, rowLogSumExps,
#>     rowMadDiffs, rowMads, rowMaxs, rowMeans2, rowMedians, rowMins,
#>     rowOrderStats, rowProds, rowQuantiles, rowRanges, rowRanks,
#>     rowSdDiffs, rowSds, rowSums2, rowTabulates, rowVarDiffs, rowVars,
#>     rowWeightedMads, rowWeightedMeans, rowWeightedMedians,
#>     rowWeightedSds, rowWeightedVars
#> Loading required package: GenomicRanges
#> Loading required package: stats4
#> Loading required package: BiocGenerics
#> Loading required package: generics
#> 
#> Attaching package: 'generics'
#> The following objects are masked from 'package:base':
#> 
#>     as.difftime, as.factor, as.ordered, intersect, is.element, setdiff,
#>     setequal, union
#> 
#> Attaching package: 'BiocGenerics'
#> The following objects are masked from 'package:stats':
#> 
#>     IQR, mad, sd, var, xtabs
#> The following objects are masked from 'package:base':
#> 
#>     anyDuplicated, aperm, append, as.data.frame, basename, cbind,
#>     colnames, dirname, do.call, duplicated, eval, evalq, Filter, Find,
#>     get, grep, grepl, is.unsorted, lapply, Map, mapply, match, mget,
#>     order, paste, pmax, pmax.int, pmin, pmin.int, Position, rank,
#>     rbind, Reduce, rownames, sapply, saveRDS, table, tapply, unique,
#>     unsplit, which.max, which.min
#> Loading required package: S4Vectors
#> 
#> Attaching package: 'S4Vectors'
#> The following object is masked from 'package:utils':
#> 
#>     findMatches
#> The following objects are masked from 'package:base':
#> 
#>     expand.grid, I, unname
#> Loading required package: IRanges
#> Loading required package: Seqinfo
#> Loading required package: Biobase
#> Welcome to Bioconductor
#> 
#>     Vignettes contain introductory material; view with
#>     'browseVignettes()'. To cite Bioconductor, see
#>     'citation("Biobase")', and for packages 'citation("pkgname")'.
#> 
#> Attaching package: 'Biobase'
#> The following object is masked from 'package:MatrixGenerics':
#> 
#>     rowMedians
#> The following objects are masked from 'package:matrixStats':
#> 
#>     anyMissing, rowMedians

se_list <- readRDS(system.file("extdata", "pooideae_vignette.rds",
                                package = "rcomplex"))
species <- names(se_list)

# Life cycle trait
trait <- c(BDIS = "annual", BSYL = "perennial",
           HVUL = "annual", HJUB = "perennial",
           BMAX = "annual", BMED = "perennial",
           VBRO = "annual", FPRA = "perennial")

Build co-expression networks for each species using Mutual Rank (MR) normalization at 3% density:

networks <- lapply(species, function(sp) {
  compute_network(se_list[[sp]], norm_method = "MR", density = 0.03)
})
names(networks) <- species

Extract ortholog pairs from HOG annotations in rowData:

ortho_pairs <- utils::combn(species, 2, simplify = FALSE)
ortho_list <- lapply(ortho_pairs, function(pair) {
  extract_orthologs(se_list[[pair[1]]], se_list[[pair[2]]])
})
orthologs <- do.call(rbind, ortho_list)
cat(nrow(orthologs), "ortholog pairs across",
    length(ortho_pairs), "species pairs\n")
#> 62152 ortholog pairs across 28 species pairs

2. Pairwise comparison

run_pairwise_comparisons() runs the full comparison pipeline for all C(8,2) = 28 species pairs: hypergeometric neighborhood test, Storey q-values, and edge classification:

edges <- run_pairwise_comparisons(networks, orthologs)
cat(nrow(edges), "edges:", sum(edges$type == "conserved"), "conserved,",
    sum(edges$type == "ns"), "non-significant\n")
#> 31735 edges: 11590 conserved, 20145 non-significant

3. Gene/HOG-level analysis

For a single species pair, summarize_comparison() provides detailed statistics. The sp1/sp2 arguments produce edge-format output directly:

comp_bdis_bsyl <- compare_neighborhoods(
  networks$BDIS, networks$BSYL, orthologs)
summary_bb <- summarize_comparison(comp_bdis_bsyl,
                                    sp1 = "BDIS", sp2 = "BSYL")
cat("Reciprocally conserved pairs:", summary_bb$summary$gene_pairs$reciprocal,
    "of", summary_bb$summary$gene_pairs$total, "\n")
#> Reciprocally conserved pairs: 311 of 847

4. Module-level analysis

Detect co-expression modules with Leiden community detection, then compare across species:

mod_bdis <- detect_modules(networks$BDIS, method = "leiden", resolution = 1.0)
mod_bsyl <- detect_modules(networks$BSYL, method = "leiden", resolution = 1.0)

mod_comp <- compare_modules(mod_bdis, mod_bsyl, orthologs,
                             method = "hypergeometric")
classes <- classify_modules(mod_comp)
print(table(classes$classification))
#> 
#>        conserved species_specific 
#>                2               46

5. Clique detection

Find fully connected subsets of ortholog groups across species:

# All cliques (at least 2 species)
cliques <- find_cliques(edges, species, min_species = 2L)
cat(nrow(cliques), "cliques\n")
#> 1044 cliques
print(table(cliques$n_species))
#> 
#>   2   3   4   5   6   7   8 
#> 610 188 103  55  32  55   1

Analytical persistence scores measure how far above the detection threshold each clique’s supporting edges are:

cliques_p <- clique_persistence(cliques, species, networks, edges)
cat("Median persistence:", round(median(cliques_p$persistence, na.rm = TRUE), 2), "\n")
#> Median persistence: 1

6. Combined robustness analysis

The strongest findings are cliques robust on two independent axes:

  • Threshold robustness – the clique survives at stricter density thresholds (the co-expression signal is strong, not marginal)
  • Phylogenetic robustness – the clique survives species removal (the conservation pattern is general, not taxon-dependent)

Network density controls how many edges are retained in the co-expression network. At 3% density, only the top 3% of all pairwise MR values pass the threshold – these are the strongest co-expression relationships. A gene pair that barely passes at 3% might not pass at 2.5%, while a truly co-expressed pair will survive much stricter cutoffs.

We test robustness by sweeping over tighter density percentiles and running leave-k-out jackknife stability at each level:

# The baseline density is 3%. Each multiplier tightens the MR threshold,
# which corresponds to a lower effective density percentile.
# Compute the actual density at each multiplier level.
baseline_density <- 0.03

multipliers <- c(1.01, 1.02, 1.03, 1.05)
robustness <- list()

for (m in multipliers) {

  # Re-threshold: only $network and $threshold are needed downstream
  tight_nets <- lapply(networks, function(net) {
    list(network = net$network, threshold = net$threshold * m)
  })
  names(tight_nets) <- species

  # Compute effective density at this threshold (fraction of edges surviving)
  eff_densities <- vapply(species, function(sp) {
    mat <- networks[[sp]]$network
    vals <- mat[upper.tri(mat)]
    mean(vals >= networks[[sp]]$threshold * m)
  }, numeric(1))

  # Re-run comparisons at stricter threshold
  tight_edges <- run_pairwise_comparisons(tight_nets, orthologs)

  # Find cliques at stricter threshold
  tight_cliques <- find_cliques(tight_edges, species, min_species = 2L)

  # Jackknife stability: test all k from 1 to max (N - 2 = 6)
  if (nrow(tight_cliques) > 0) {
    max_k <- length(species) - 2L
    stab <- clique_stability(tight_edges, species, trait,
                              all_species = species,
                              full_cliques = tight_cliques,
                              max_k = max_k)
    robustness[[as.character(m)]] <- list(
      cliques = tight_cliques,
      n_cliques = nrow(tight_cliques),
      n_exclusive = length(stab$stability_class),
      stability_class = stab$stability_class,
      mean_density = mean(eff_densities),
      stability = stab
    )
  } else {
    robustness[[as.character(m)]] <- list(
      cliques = tight_cliques, n_cliques = 0L, n_exclusive = 0L,
      stability_class = integer(0),
      mean_density = mean(eff_densities))
  }
}

# Summary: cliques surviving at each density level with stability class
# stability_class = highest k at which ALL species-removal subsets preserve
# the clique's trait exclusivity. k=6 means the clique survives removal of
# any 6 of 8 species (maximally robust).
max_k <- length(species) - 2L
header <- paste0("Density    | Cliques | Exclusive | ",
                 paste(sprintf("k=%d", 0:max_k), collapse = " | "))
cat(header, "\n")
#> Density    | Cliques | Exclusive | k=0 | k=1 | k=2 | k=3 | k=4 | k=5 | k=6
cat(paste(rep("-", nchar(header)), collapse = ""), "\n")
#> --------------------------------------------------------------------------
for (m in names(robustness)) {
  r <- robustness[[m]]
  sc_tab <- table(factor(r$stability_class, levels = 0:max_k))
  sc_str <- paste(sprintf("%3d", sc_tab), collapse = " | ")
  cat(sprintf("%-10s | %7d | %9d | %s\n",
              sprintf("%.2f%%", r$mean_density * 100),
              r$n_cliques, r$n_exclusive, sc_str))
}
#> 2.38%      |     992 |       260 |  28 |   0 |   0 |   0 |   0 |   0 | 232
#> 1.81%      |     900 |       254 |  26 |   0 |   0 |   0 |   0 |   0 | 228
#> 1.31%      |     777 |       199 |  18 |   0 |   0 |   0 |   0 |   0 | 181
#> 0.46%      |     429 |       128 |  11 |   0 |   0 |   0 |   0 |   0 | 117

The stability class distribution is strikingly bimodal: cliques are either unstable (class 0) or maximally stable (class 6). This makes biological sense – with 4 species per trait group, a trait-exclusive clique either depends on a specific species combination (fragile) or reflects a genuinely conserved pattern that persists regardless of which species are removed (robust).

Cliques that survive at tighter density AND have maximal stability class represent the strongest conservation signals in the dataset.

7. HOG classification

Classify each HOG by its conservation pattern using the waterfall: complete > partial > differentiated > trait_specific > unclassified.

# Use stability from the 1.02x level (tighter than baseline, with data)
stab_input <- robustness[["1.02"]]$stability

result <- classify_cliques(edges, species, trait,
                            min_species = 2L,
                            stability = stab_input)
print(table(result$classification))
#> 
#>       complete differentiated        partial trait_specific   unclassified 
#>              1              5            402             30             59
  • complete: all 8 species form a clique – deeply conserved across all Pooideae
  • partial: clique spans both annual and perennial species, but not all 8
  • differentiated: annual species have a clique AND perennial species have a clique, but no cross-group conservation – lineage-specific co-expression programs
  • trait_specific: only annuals or only perennials have a clique
  • unclassified: no conservation signal detected
